---
date: "2026-07-27"
---

## Data Governance & Week Wrap-Up

- Part 8 — data governance with legal teeth, plus week wrap-up

::: notes
Welcome back to Reading Guide Pack Six and the AI Lab Manual, part eight of eight. In part seven we covered three capstone commitments: disclose the assistance, make API work reproducible, and guard against leakage. Now we cover the fourth and most consequential rule, the one with actual legal teeth — data governance — and then we pull the whole week together. This is the rule a mentor will not bend on, because breaking it is not a quality problem, it is a license violation that can put the program's data access at risk.
:::

## Licensed Data Stays Home

- Per CONVENTIONS §5: CRSP, Compustat, **TRACE**, WRDS stay read-only on GMU
- Sending licensed rows to an external API is a **license violation**
- **Never** paste a CRSP return, a TRACE trade, or any non-redistributable row
- Not a quality issue — a **legal and data-governance** breach
- This is the rule a mentor will not bend on

::: notes
Fourth, obey the data-governance rule — this one has legal teeth. Per conventions section five, licensed data — CRSP, Compustat, TRACE, anything sourced from WRDS — stays read-only on GMU infrastructure and never leaves it. Sending licensed rows to an external API, such as a public endpoint from Anthropic or OpenAI, is a license violation and a data-governance breach, full stop. So never paste licensed or sensitive data into an external LLM API — not a CRSP return, not a TRACE bond trade, not a single row you are not allowed to redistribute. This is not a quality problem you can patch later; it is a legal one, and it is the rule a mentor will simply not bend on, because a breach can jeopardize the data access the whole program depends on.
:::

## How to Use Models Safely

- For sensitive data, run the model **locally**
- **Ollama** on your machine, or a **GMU Hopper A100** node (SLURM, Appendix B.4)
- Data and model both stay inside the **licensed perimeter**
- **GMU Azure APIM** = in-perimeter hosted option, when the agreement covers it
- External API? Send only **public** text — EDGAR 10-K yes, joined CRSP no

::: notes
So how do you use a model on sensitive data safely? Run it locally. Use Ollama on your own machine, or a GMU Hopper A-one-hundred node using the SLURM template in Appendix B-point-four, where the data and the model both stay inside the licensed perimeter. The GMU Azure APIM gateway is the in-perimeter option for hosted models when GMU's agreement covers it. When you must use an external API, send only non-licensed public text — a ten-K from EDGAR is public and fine; the CRSP return you joined to it is not, so strip to the public columns first before anything leaves the perimeter.
:::

## The Whole Week in One Thread

- Read the five papers asking **"is the measure valid?"**
- Build your **RAG** over public 10-Ks
- Write prompts that **constrain and cite**
- Score every LLM label on a held-out gold set: precision, recall, $F_1$, kappa
- Carry it into a capstone you can hand a skeptic and **defend line by line**

::: notes
Let me pull the whole week into one thread. A result is only as good as your ability to reproduce it and defend it. A language model does not change that standard; it raises the bar, because it adds a stochastic, opaque, license-sensitive instrument to your toolkit. So log it, pin it, validate it, disclose it, and keep the licensed data home — and the co-pilot makes you faster without making you wrong. Read the five Week-Six papers asking is the measure valid, not is the effect causal. Build your retrieval system over public ten-Ks. Write prompts that constrain and cite. Score every LLM label against a held-out gold set with precision, recall, F-one, and kappa. And carry all of it into a capstone you can hand a skeptic and defend line by line.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Licensed data — CRSP, Compustat, TRACE, WRDS — **never leaves GMU**; run models locally (Ollama, Hopper) or send only public text to an external API. The skeptic who grades a stranger's measurement table and the researcher who validates her own LLM labels are the same person. Next, in Weeks 7–8, you build the capstone.
:::

::: notes
To close the AI Lab Manual and Week Six: the data-governance rule is the one with legal teeth — licensed data from CRSP, Compustat, TRACE, and WRDS never leaves GMU infrastructure, so run models locally on Ollama or Hopper when the data is sensitive, and send only public text like an EDGAR ten-K to any external API. Underneath every rule this week is the same principle the whole book defends: a result is only as good as your ability to reproduce and defend it. The skeptic who reads a stranger's measurement table and the researcher who validates her own LLM labels are, once again, the same person. Next, in Weeks Seven and Eight, you build the capstone where all of this comes together — measurement, retrieval, prompting, validation, and disclosure, in one project you can defend line by line.
:::
